retrieved_docs.title: Dysphagia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral, cerebellar, or brainstem strokes can impair swallowing function. These lesions can interrupt voluntary control of mastication and bolus transport during the oropharyngeal phase. Furthermore, cortical lesions involving the precentral gyrus cause contralateral impairment of facial muscles, lip and tongue movements, and pharyngeal peristalsis. Brainstem lesions are less common but result in significant swallowing compromise due to dysfunction of the cranial nerves emerging from the brainstem. Mouth, tongue, and cheek sensations may be compromised. Alterations may occur in the timing of pharyngeal swallow, laryngeal elevation, glottic closure, and physiological regulation of oropharyngeal swallow. [25] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Swallowing Study -- Potential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 780,000 individuals in the US have a stroke in any given year, with aspiration pneumonia affecting one-third of stroke sufferers. [9] This is most often a result of abnormal/absent swallow reflex, inefficient lip closure, and limited tongue movement due to stroke-related cranial neuropathies. Therefore, providing nutrition via a tube for the first few weeks of a recent stroke is imperative if the patient cannot maintain adequate oral intake. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Swallowing Study -- Normal and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Poor oral control can be demonstrated in videofluoroscopy by material falling through the lips (eg, neuropathies), the inability of the subject to form a bolus using their tongue and to subsequently correctly position it in the oral cavity (eg, stroke), or by a lack of seal formation (posterior tongue not adequately contacting the soft palate) leading to leakage of material and possible laryngeal penetration and aspiration. Poor tongue coordination or hesitant initiation of swallowing can show stasis of material within the oral cavity. Failure of soft palate elevation during swallowing subsequently leads to an insufficient palatopharyngeal seal, resulting in nasopharyngeal regurgitation.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Stroke (Nursing) -- Assessment
retrieved_docs.content: A thorough history is a critical first step toward making a diagnosis. A stroke should be high on the differential for a patient presenting with sudden, focal neurological deficits and/or an altered level of consciousness. Based on clinical presentation alone, it is almost impossible to differentiate between a hemorrhagic and an ischemic stroke. Common signs and symptoms include hemiparesis, sensory deficits, diplopia, dysarthria, and facial droop. More posterior strokes present with a sudden onset of ataxia and vertigo. Symptoms commonly attributed to increased intracranial pressure, such as nausea, vomiting, headache, and blurred or double vision, may provide evidence supporting a hemorrhagic stroke. In addition to gathering information about the symptoms, one needs to establish the time of their onset to decide whether fibrinolytic therapy is an option once the diagnosis of ischemic stroke is confirmed. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Bell Palsy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: When Bell palsy occurs, patients typically present complaining of weakness and often "numbness" of one side of the face, usually preceded 1 to 2 days prior by a dull ache behind or within the ipsilateral ear. Patients may report a dry eye over the course of the day prior to presentation, noting drooling while brushing their teeth before going to bed, and potentially numbness of the tongue or a metallic taste in the mouth. Some patients will also describe ipsilateral ear pain with loud noises. Upon awakening, the patient or partner will frequently notice facial asymmetry and may be concerned about the possibility of a stroke, which often leads to an emergency department visit. Generally, however, distinguishing between a stroke and Bell palsy is very straightforward, and patients can be reassured and discharged with steroids, antivirals, and eye lubricant as long as there is no suspicion for Ramsay Hunt syndrome, Lyme disease, or any other conditions that may cause a facial nerve palsy.

retrieved_docs.title: Cerebrovascular Disease (Nursing) -- Assessment
retrieved_docs.content: The stroke exam is a multi-person coordinated rapid exam.  While staff obtain vitals, attach telemetry, and obtain IV access, the physician performs rapid neurological evaluation.  National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale (NIHSS) is routinely used to get the baseline evaluation. The exam has to be rapid as “time is brain.”  One must examine the following items: The level of consciousness (alert and responsive, arouses to noxious stimuli, comatose…) Language (fluency, naming, comprehension, repetition) Dysarthria (slurring) which may be picked up in the history Motor (subtle arm weakness can be picked up by performing a pronator drift) Visual field deficits Eye movement abnormalities (in general if a gaze preference is present, the eyes deviate towards the side of the lesion) Facial paralysis (asking the patient to smile) Ataxia (finger to nose) With a good history and physical exam, we can localize the stroke. There are various stroke syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: Brainstem Stroke -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: ACT FAST is an acronym suggested by the American Stroke Association to recognize the early symptoms of a stroke and consists of the following components: F -Face drooping A -Arm Weakness S -Speech T -Time to call 911 Along with the above symptoms, if the patient experiences any of the following, emergency medical services must be activated: Sudden confusion Sudden trouble seeing Sudden numbness Sudden trouble walking Sudden severe headache Managing risk factors can significantly reduce future strokes, including: Smoking cessation Alcohol use Drug addiction and abuse Hypertension and diabetes control Obesity and a sedentary lifestyle Obstructive sleep apnea syndrome [81]

retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Artery Infarct -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Counsel the patient and family regarding stroke that the risk of a stroke can be decreased by appropriately treating diabetes, hypertension, high cholesterol, cardiac diseases, and obesity, quitting smoking, reducing alcohol intake, and staying physically active. Discuss the BE-FAST (balance loss, eyesight, facial droop, arm weakness, slurry speech, time) symptoms awareness on all the media and public education channels and campaigns. Time is of the essence if they notice symptomatic onset.

retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Artery Thrombosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Rapid onset of advanced motor and bulbar symptoms with a decreased level of consciousness. Insidious or stuttering symptoms over a few days as a combination of the above that end with disabling motor and bulbar symptoms, a decreased level of consciousness, or both. Prodromal symptoms may include headache, neck pain, loss of vision, binocular diplopia, dysarthria, dizziness, hemiparesis, paresthesias, ataxia, and tonic-clonic type movements. “Herald hemiparesis” is the phrase to describe the momentary, unilateral weakness that may precede later permanent symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Cerebrovascular Disease -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The stroke exam is a multi-person coordinated rapid exam.  While staff obtain vitals, attach telemetry, and obtain IV access, the physician performs a rapid neurological evaluation.  National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale (NIHSS) is routinely used to get the baseline evaluation. The exam has to be rapid as “time is brain.”  One must examine the following items: The level of consciousness (alert and responsive, arouses to noxious stimuli, comatose…) Language (fluency, naming, comprehension, repetition) Dysarthria (slurring) which may be picked up in the history Motor (subtle arm weakness can be picked up by performing a pronator drift) Visual field deficits Eye movement abnormalities (in general if a gaze preference is present, the eyes deviate towards the side of the lesion) Facial paralysis (asking the patient to smile) Ataxia (finger to nose) With a good history and physical exam, we can localize the stroke. There are various stroke syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Cerebral Artery Stroke -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: "BEFAST" is a good and handy acronym to recognize common signs of a stroke, regardless of the etiology. Stroke is an emergency. Time is brain. B alance- Is there a sudden onset loss of balance or coordination? E ye- Is there a sudden onset blurred or double vision or any sudden onset, persistent vision problems? F ace- When the person smiles, does one or both sides of the face hang limply? A rms- Ask the person to raise both arms. Does one side slope downward? Is there a sudden onset weakness/numbness on one side? S peech- Does the person have slurred or garbled speech? Can the patient repeat simple phrases? T ime- Call 911 for immediate medical attention upon noticing one or more or these signs. Also, one should take note of when the symptoms began. To help prevent strokes, one should:

retrieved_docs.title: Cerebrovascular Disease -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: F (Face) - A droop or an uneven smile on a person’s face. A (Arms) - Arm numbness or weakness - Elicited by asking the patient to lift the arms S (Speech difficulty) - Slurred speech or difficulty in understanding speech T (Time) - If any of the above features are present, even if transient, it is time to call the emergency helpline (911). The additional symptoms of stroke to be watched for are: Sudden Numbness Sudden Confusion Sudden Trouble Seeing Sudden Trouble Walking Sudden Severe Headache

retrieved_docs.title: Peripheral Magnetic Stimulation -- Clinical Significance -- Dysphagia
retrieved_docs.content: Eight patients with stroke and dysphagia (7 out of 8 patients had subcortical strokes) were reported to have some reduction in penetration-aspiration episodes after applying additional PMS to swallowing exercises for a week. Although, no single patient could change their mode of nutritional intake. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Swallowing -- Mechanism -- Pharyngeal Phase
retrieved_docs.content: The bolus is moved inferiorly by a peristaltic-like sequential contraction of the superior, middle, and inferior pharyngeal constrictor muscles in a top to bottom fashion. [9] A pattern of rapid stimulation and inhibition in the pharyngeal muscles creates a cranial-caudal wave of pressure that directs the food bolus towards the UES. [3] The speed is remarkably fast, occurring at rates of 20 to 40cm/s. [10] Once initiated, the pharyngeal phase behaves in an ‘all or none’ fashion, much like a reflex, hence why this phase is considered an irreversible step. 5.) Transit through UES

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Brain -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Parietal lobe: Damage to the frontal aspect of the parietal lobe may lead to impaired sensation and numbness on the contralateral side of the body. An individual may have difficulty recognizing texture and shape and may be unable to identify a sensation and its location on their body. Damage to the middle aspect of the parietal lobe can lead to right-left disorientation and difficulty with proprioception. Damage to the non-dominant (right) parietal lobe may lead to apraxia (difficulty with performing purposeful motions such as combing hair or brushing teeth) and difficulty with spatial orientation and navigation (they may get lost in a once familiar area). Patients with non-dominant parietal lobe damage, usually from a middle cerebral artery stroke, may neglect the side opposite of the brain damage (usually the left side), which may manifest as only shaving the right side of their face or drawing a clock with all of the numbers on the right side of the circle. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Swallowing -- Mechanism -- Oral Stage
retrieved_docs.content: The oral stage is often split into two steps: 1.) Oral preparatory

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 231
retrieved_docs.content: surgical removal of atherosclerotic plaque—endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels (استئصال باطنة الشريان)—is indicated in the context of arterial thrombosis or embolism. The intracranial arterial structure can reveal intracerebral atherosclerotic changes, providing an indirect site for such procedures. Together, these entities illustrate the interconnected pathophysiology of precerebral arterial disease, basilar artery involvement, cerebral infarction, transient ischemic events, clinical presentation, and both medical and surgical management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 248
retrieved_docs.content: distinguishing non‑infarcting stenotic disease. The clinical course descriptor “Transitory” is applied to transient cerebral ischemia, which corresponds to a transient ischemic attack (نوبة إقفارية عابرة), highlighting the temporary nature of some ischemic events. Overall, occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries is a pivotal pathological mechanism that can lead to multiple forms of cerebral infarction, may be asymptomatic, is associated with intracranial arterial sclerosis, and is integrated within a structured classification of cerebrovascular circulatory diseases. These relationships inform diagnosis, risk assessment, therapeutic decision‑making (including endarterectomy), and the broader understanding of disease hierarchy in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2673
retrieved_docs.content: The basilar artery (arteria basilaris) is identified as a common site where transient cerebral ischemia can occur. Transient cerebral ischemia typically follows a transitory clinical course, indicating that its symptoms are brief and reversible. In Arabic, transient cerebral ischemia is referred to as “نوبة إقفارية عابرة,” underscoring its nature as a short‑lasting ischemic episode.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1728
retrieved_docs.content: Late effects of cerebrovascular disease has parent اضطراب مخي وعائي, indicating that the long‑term sequelae of cerebrovascular disease are classified under the broader category of cerebrovascular disorder. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians organize diagnoses, anticipate chronic complications, and tailor long‑term management strategies for patients who have experienced cerebrovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 188
retrieved_docs.content: arterial occlusion, stenosis, thrombosis, embolism, and chronic atherosclerosis converge to produce both acute cerebral infarcts and transient ischemic attacks, underscoring their relevance for diagnosis, risk assessment, and management of cerebrovascular disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2452
retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia, referred to in Arabic as نوبة إقفارية عابرة, denotes a brief, reversible episode of reduced blood flow to a focal area of the brain that does not result in permanent tissue damage. This condition is characterized by temporary neurological deficits that resolve within minutes to hours and serves as an important clinical warning sign for the potential development of a full‑blown stroke. Recognizing the relationship between transient cerebral ischemia and نوبة إقفارية عابرة is essential for prompt evaluation, risk stratification, and implementation of preventive strategies to reduce the likelihood of subsequent cerebrovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2126
retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia is characterized by a transitory clinical course, indicating that the neurological deficits associated with this brief interruption of cerebral blood flow are temporary and typically resolve quickly without permanent damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2668
retrieved_docs.content: embolie; arteria basilaris, referred to in Arabic as انصمام الشريان المخي, denotes an embolic occlusion of the basilar artery. This condition occurs when a thrombus or other embolic material blocks the basilar artery, which supplies blood to the brainstem, cerebellum, and posterior cerebral circulation. The resulting ischemia can lead to severe neurological deficits, including loss of consciousness, cranial nerve palsies, motor weakness, and potentially fatal outcomes if not promptly recognized and treated. Early diagnosis through neuroimaging and rapid reperfusion strategies are critical for improving patient prognosis and minimizing long‑term disability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2302
retrieved_docs.content: Migraine with ischemic complication is a broader clinical category that encompasses forms of migraine in which vascular ischemia occurs, and persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction is a specific manifestation within this category. Persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction is also classified as an acute cerebrovascular disease, reflecting its nature as a sudden, stroke‑like event caused by interruption of cerebral blood flow during a prolonged migraine aura. By linking migraine with ischemic complication to acute cerebrovascular disease through the specific entity of persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction, the knowledge underscores that certain prolonged migraine auras can evolve into acute cerebral infarctions, highlighting the importance of recognizing these migraine‑related ischemic complications for timely diagnosis and appropriate stroke‑focused management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 162
retrieved_docs.content: occlusion or stenosis—whether symptomatic, asymptomatic, thrombotic, embolic, or associated with intracranial arterial sclerosis—is the central pathogenic substrate that leads to cerebral (and occasionally cardiac) infarction, informs diagnostic imaging, and guides interventions such as endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 175
retrieved_docs.content: in cerebraal infarct,” highlights that precerebral arterial narrowing does not always produce overt infarction. Asymptomatic basilar‑artery occlusion may be interpreted within the context of “General wellbeing” and can manifest subtly as syncope (“غشي”), a transient clinical sign that also connects to “Transient cerebral ischemia” (a child of “Circulatory Diseases and Syndromes of Nervous System”). The term “Transitory” links both syncope and transient cerebral ischemia, indicating that brief, reversible ischemic episodes (transient ischemic attacks نوبة إقفارية عابرة) may precede permanent infarction. Imaging and pathological findings such as intracranial atherosclerosis (“تصلب عصيدي في المخ”) are mapped from precerebral arterial disease and are located in the “Structure of intracranial artery.” This structural involvement provides an indirect procedural site for endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels (“endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck”), which falls under the broader

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral infarction (“الاحتشاء”) results from occlusion, stenosis, embolism or thrombosis of the precerebral arteries, including embolic or thrombotic precerebral artery infarcts and basilar‑artery occlusion, and is pathophysiologically linked to “انسداد الأوعية الدموية” (vascular occlusion). Asymptomatic basilar‑artery occlusion, although sometimes perceived as a state of general wellbeing, may present with transient faintness (“غشي”). Within the hierarchy of nervous‑system circulatory disorders, “Circulatory Diseases and Syndromes of Nervous System” contains the child “حادثة وعائية دماغية” (cerebrovascular accident) and the parent “transient cerebral ischemia” (TIA, “نوبة إقفارية عابرة”), the latter being a brief, reversible reduction in cerebral blood flow. Cerebral artery sclerosis (“تصلب الشرايين المخية”) and intracranial arterial sclerosis (“تصلب عصيدي في المخ”) contribute to chronic arterial stiffening, increase the risk of cerebrovascular accidents, and are associated with

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1831
retrieved_docs.content: Transitory denotes a clinical course that encompasses episodes of غشي and characterizes transient cerebral ischemia. Transient cerebral ischemia, which is also referred to by the Arabic term نوبة إقفارية عابرة, shares this transitory pattern, linking the concept of fleeting neurological impairment with the manifestation of غشي. Understanding this relationship highlights that both غشي and نوبة إقفارية عابرة represent brief, reversible events within a transitory clinical trajectory.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1020
retrieved_docs.content: Asymptomatic occlusion of the basilar artery is evaluated in relation to a patient’s general wellbeing, a concept that is further interpreted by the term غشي. The term غشي, which denotes a transient clinical state, is also described as the clinical course of a transitory episode. Thus, the presence of an asymptomatic basilar artery occlusion may be understood through the lens of general wellbeing and may manifest as a brief, transient episode characterized by غشي.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: situates these processes in the basilar artery (Arteria basilaris) and the broader Structure of intracranial artery, which serves as a finding site for occlusion/stenosis, transient cerebral ischemia, and intracranial atherosclerosis, and as an indirect procedure site for endarterectomy of head and neck vessels (endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck) under the broader procedure category endarterectomy (استئصال باطنة الشريان). This integrated network supports comprehensive assessment and management of precerebral arterial disease, its neurologic manifestations, and systemic consequences.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia is described as having a transitory clinical course, indicating that its neurological deficits are brief and resolve quickly. This condition corresponds to the Arabic term نوبة إقفارية عابرة, which denotes the same short‑lasting, reversible episode of cerebral ischemia.
